g.data-narration: Welcome to System Models for Cloud Computing. We'll explore cluster, grid, and cloud computing models.
System models provide abstract representations of distributed computing systems, helping us understand their structure and behavior.
g.text: System models provide abstract representations of distributed computing systems,
helping understand structure, behavior, and key characteristics in cloud environments.

g.data-narration: Distributed systems have seven key characteristics: concurrency, no global clock, independent failures, resource sharing, openness, transparency, and scalability.
Computing evolved from traditional localized systems through cluster, grid, and finally cloud computing with on-demand resources.
Cluster computing uses tightly coupled homogeneous systems with centralized management and high-speed interconnects for HPC applications.
g.text: Tightly coupled | Homogeneous | Centralized | HPC Examples: Beowulf, HPC Clusters

g.data-narration: Grid computing provides loosely coupled heterogeneous resources across organizations with decentralized control for resource sharing.
g.text: Loosely coupled | Heterogeneous | Decentralized | Examples: TeraGrid, EGEE

g.data-narration: Cloud computing has five NIST characteristics: on-demand, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.
g.text: NIST Cloud Definition: Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool.

g.data-narration: Compare the three models: clusters are tight-coupled with high homogeneity, grids are loose-coupled with heterogeneity, and cloud uses variable coupling with SLA-based service.
g.text: Comparison: Cluster vs Grid vs Cloud Computing

g.data-narration: IaaS provides virtualized computing resources - you manage applications, data, runtime, middleware, and OS while provider handles virtualization and hardware.
PaaS provides development platforms - provider manages everything except applications and data while developers focus on code.
SaaS delivers complete software over the web - provider manages everything from hardware to applications, users just use the software.
Cloud deployment models include public, private, hybrid, and community clouds - each serving different organizational needs.
Cloud computing is enabled by virtualization, web services, distributed storage, and automation tools like Kubernetes and Terraform.
Key takeaways: Understand cluster, grid, cloud differences; know 5 NIST characteristics; remember IaaS, PaaS, SaaS layers with examples; know deployment models.
g.text: • Understand differences: Cluster (tight, HPC), Grid (loose, distributed), Cloud (on-demand)
• Remember 5 NIST characteristics: On-demand, Broad access, Pooling, Elasticity, Measured
• IaaS: EC2, Azure VMs | PaaS: App Engine, Heroku | SaaS: Office 365, Salesforce
